course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
title: Diagonalization and Matrix Powers
sessionGoal: Students should be able to write A=PDP^{-1} when enough eigenvectors exist and use it to compute powers efficiently.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start with the sentence: diagonalization changes to an eigenvector coordinate system.
Do Problem 1 as the main full diagonalization example.
If time is short, skip the symbolic formula and compute A^3 only.
Use Problem 2 to show powers acting on a vector without multiplying matrices repeatedly.
sayThisFirst: The columns of P are eigenvectors.
The diagonal entries of D are the matching eigenvalues in the same order.
Matrix powers become easier because A^n=PD^nP^{-1}.
boardPlan: Write the diagonalization recipe: eigenvalues, eigenvectors, build P and D.
Emphasize matching order: column 1 of P matches entry 1 of D.
Use D^n to compute a power.
Do the vector power problem if time remains.
keyFormulas: D = diagonal matrix of matching eigenvalues.
A is diagonalizable when it has enough independent eigenvectors.
commonMistakes: Putting eigenvalues in D in an order that does not match P.
Using A^n=P D P^{-1}^n instead of A^n=P D^n P^{-1}.
notationWarnings: If P=[v_1 v_2], then D=diag(λ_1,λ_2) in that same order.
Use A^n=PD^nP^{-1}; the P and P^{-1} do not get powers.

problems.title: Diagonalize a 2 by 2 matrix and compute A^3
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \[A=\begin{bmatrix}4&2\\1&3\end{bmatrix}.\] Use the eigenvectors \(v_1=(2,1)\) for \(\lambda_1=5\) and \(v_2=(1,-1)\) for \(\lambda_2=2\) to diagonalize \(A\). Then compute \(A^3\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: the eigenvectors and eigenvalues are already given, so the main issue is organizing P and D correctly.

drawInstructions: Draw A as complicated in standard coordinates.
Draw P^{-1} as changing into eigenvector coordinates.
Draw D as simple stretching by 5 and 2.

walkthrough.label: Build P and D in matching order

commonMistakes: Putting the eigenvectors as rows of P instead of columns.
Putting 2 before 5 in D while leaving P unchanged.
Computing A^3 by multiplying A three times even though diagonalization is available.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 6 for diagonalization and powers.

problems.title: Use eigenvectors to compute A^n x
problems.studentPromptLatex: Suppose \(A v_1=3v_1\) for \(v_1=(1,1)\), and \(A v_2=-v_2\) for \(v_2=(1,-1)\). Let \(x=(5,1)\). Find a formula for \(A^n x\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: write x in the eigenvector basis. Then powers just power the eigenvalues.

drawInstructions: Draw x as a combination of the two eigenvector directions.
Label the v1 component gets multiplied by 3^n.
Label the v2 component gets multiplied by (-1)^n.

walkthrough.label: Write x as a combination of eigenvectors
Solve for the coefficients
walkthrough.latex: This gives \(c_1+c_2=5\) and \(c_1-c_2=1\). Adding gives \(2c_1=6\), so \(c_1=3\). Then \(c_2=2\).

walkthrough.label: Apply A^n to each eigenvector piece

commonMistakes: Trying to find A explicitly even though it is not needed.
Forgetting to decompose x into eigenvectors first.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 6 for powers using diagonalization.

problems.title: Diagonalize a triangular matrix with distinct eigenvalues
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \[C=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&2&1\\0&0&3\end{bmatrix}.\] Diagonalize \(C\) by finding matrices \(P\) and \(D\) such that \(C=PDP^{-1}\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: distinct eigenvalues usually make diagonalization straightforward, but we still need eigenvectors.

drawInstructions: Circle the diagonal entries to get eigenvalues.
Write one eigenvector under each eigenvalue.
Build P with those eigenvectors as columns.

walkthrough.label: Find eigenvalues
walkthrough.latex: The matrix is triangular, so the eigenvalues are \(1,2,3\).

walkthrough.label: Find eigenvector for λ=1
walkthrough.latex: \[C-I=\begin{bmatrix}0&0&0\\0&1&1\\0&0&2\end{bmatrix}.\] This gives \(z=0\) and \(y=0\), so one eigenvector is \((1,0,0)\).

walkthrough.label: Find eigenvector for λ=2
walkthrough.latex: \[C-2I=\begin{bmatrix}-1&0&0\\0&0&1\\0&0&1\end{bmatrix}.\] This gives \(x=0\), \(z=0\), with \(y\) free. One eigenvector is \((0,1,0)\).

walkthrough.label: Find eigenvector for λ=3
walkthrough.latex: \[C-3I=\begin{bmatrix}-2&0&0\\0&-1&1\\0&0&0\end{bmatrix}.\] This gives \(x=0\) and \(-y+z=0\), so \(z=y\). One eigenvector is \((0,1,1)\).

walkthrough.label: Build P and D
walkthrough.latex: Use the eigenvectors in the same order as the eigenvalues: \[P=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&1\\0&0&1\end{bmatrix},\qquad D=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&2&0\\0&0&3\end{bmatrix}.\]

commonMistakes: Reading eigenvalues correctly but skipping eigenvectors.
Putting eigenvectors in P in a different order than D.
Assuming D is the original diagonal of C and forgetting P.

wrapUpQuestions: Why is D^n easy to compute?
What order mistake breaks the diagonalization?

linksToWebsite.note: Practice Lab Section(s) 6 for more practice.

qualityControlNotes: Checked P inverse for 2x2 example and A^3 = [[86,78],[39,47]].
reviewSummary: Session 13 turns eigenvectors into a practical tool for powers.
